sentences.text: In the center?
OK.
Should I take this here?
OK.
To identify, yes.
I'm Brenda Senders.
I was born in Poland it was at that time, [? at ?] the Ukraine.
The name of the town was Sarny.
Sarny, S-A-R-N-Y.
It was August 20th, 1925.
It's-- I remember [?
Brendel. ?] At birth my first name was [?
Brendel. ?] [?
Bronia ?] they called me in Polish.
Was Szyr, S-Z-Y-R.
My father's first name was Samuel.
In the late-- in the beginning of 1900 or 1898, I'm not-- I'm not sure of that.
He was born not far from Sarny.
I-- as I remember it, my recollection what I was told, in [?
Karminey, ?] [?
Karminey ?] they called it.
It's a small-- as far as I know it, [?
Karminey ?] was a-- a village, or it was a small little town.
I don't know.
And he came from a very fine family, as I understood at that time.
My mother's first name was Leah.
Approximately maybe a year younger than my father.
I don't know, maybe the same.
I-- the same, I would call it the same.
Let's say in 1900.
She was born near Sarny, in a small-- it's like a village, [?
Karpilufka ?].
I think that's what is the name, yeah, [?
Karpilufka. ?]
Maiden family name was Borko.
I had two sisters and a brother.
My sister's name was Sima.
The other one was Esther.
And my brother's name was Solomon, [?
Shalomke ?] they called him.
As far as I-- Sima is in 1933, I think September.
And my little brother was approximately 1936, sister 1938.
One sister, Sima, who was from 1933, she survived the war with me.
No.
They were killed, together with my little brother and my little sister, was murdered.
They died in 1942, [? approximately ?] in August.
They murdered them, actually.
They didn't die.
My grandfather.
My grandfather was Ziskin Szyr.
And he was quite a modern-- in his time was a very modern man.
He used to travel a lot.
And he was, at one point he was telling me there was-- he was well-to-do man.
And then when the Bolshevik came, and the Communists, they took away from him.
And he used be a traveler.
He used to travel to Austria, he told me, and to Germany.
And he met the-- the Kaiser.
He used to tell me, I was fascinated with his little stories as a kid, fascinated.
No.
I had-- yes.
I don't know.
I can show you.
I [INAUDIBLE].
I would call it in the 60s.
They died the same day, yes.
My father was seasonal.
He was a forester, like buying of quarter of-- of woods.
And then buying them off from-- from-- from-- it was owned by the government, from the [NON-ENGLISH], the-- the foresters.
Buying them off and then on-- cut-- cut them down, and then in the winter, they used to come with the slate, I remember, peasants.
I don't know, whole bunches, like a whole-- one after the other, a train of them.
They used to come, they used to load those trees.
And they used to, by train, bring 'em to Sarny.
And from Sarny, they used to load 'em on-- on-- on transports.
And they used to take 'em to Gdynia, to Gdansk, I don't know.
This is I don't know.
But this is as the-- as far as I can understand.
Because I was a school girl, and I was not interested in all those things.
It was-- in the beginning was Sarny.
I remember we lived-- we lived on [?
Olitza ?] [?
Hondlova-- ?] [?
Olitza ?] [?
Spulna ?], I'm sorry.
Olitza ?] [?
Spulna ?], they call it.
That means the street, [?
Olitza ?] is in Polish, [?
Olitza ?] is street, [?
Spulna. ?] That's the way they called the street.
I don't know the address.
I don't remember the number.
It was a little small house.
It was a apartment, I don't know.
Little homes, you know, it's a small town.
So there were homes and-- and our--
We consisted of three rooms, something, we had to share the kitchen or something.
We lived in the town of Sarny, till my father lost-- it was a seasonal-- lost his job, and the time was very rough.
And in-- in the '30s, I don't remember, '35, we moved into Rudnia, Rudnia, Rudnia.
It's a village about 15-- about 16 kilometers from Sarny.
And there my-- we opened a little little, like a grocery store.
And we used to-- my mother used to bake breads and this, and we used to sell it.
And that's how we made a living.
And my father, had again, if he had a job, he used to accept, because it was in his field.
And he was doing the buying, and-- and again, doing the same.
And he knew the peasants.
And they loved him, my father.
They used-- they liked him.
I-- I know as a fact, when I escaped from-- I escaped in the tough times.
And I told them that I am [NON-ENGLISH], that means Samuel's daughter, they used to cry over me, and give me food.
And he was-- he was helping people.
Yes, I was a student.
I went to a Tarbut gymnasium they call it.
It's a Hebrew-- and it was acknowledged by the government, of course.
And we were checked, you know.
Like inspectors used to come and check out if we have the curriculum and everything is there.
And it was a Hebrew gymnasium.
Predominately Hebrew [? it was. ?] If they were-- if they were Orthodox, my mother was more so than my father.
My father was a very modern, very updated.
You know.
He was--
He was very modern.
What?
Not regularly, but to we used to go to shul, especially on the holidays.
In Sarny, yes.
We-- I remember my father-- and I remember [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
Especially when the kids used to come, I remember going with my father.
And they called it this-- this-- this synagogue [NON-ENGLISH] Shul.
You know, this is a commerce.
All people who were in commerce or something, they belonged under or something, I don't know.
But this was the shul.
And I liked-- I remember liked very much to go with my father whenever.
The name of the rabbi, no.
But the name of the second shul, I remember, Rabbi [?
Karlinkski, ?] I think-- Kunda, I'm sorry, Rabbi Kunda.
No.
I went to school.
And a few times I was dragged to go to-- to-- with-- with my friends to come with them, and this was like a [?
Gordonia ?] or something.
We used to dance and this, and horahs.
And-- and that was appealing for a certain time.
And then I did not proceed.
I was busy with my school.
Was busy with all these things, with whatever was going on.
So I did not.
I know from my family, a lot even went as-- as pioneers, went to Palestine at that time, so my aunt, and some of my uncles.
Yes.
We were-- we are talking about the '30s, you understand.
We heard-- we heard bad news.
And we heard-- because it started, and it was imported, things from the Germans.
Then all of a sudden, we had a Madame [?
Priester ?] I remember, Madame [?
Priester ?] who was the one who decided.
She started brewing trouble in Poland.
She was in-- like a import from the Germans.
And all of a sudden, they decided, because by the Jewish law you have to slaughter a certain way a chicken and a cow and whatever.
She decided it's too-- it's too painful or it's too cruel, whatever.
They have to kill it by a bullet.
And this is against the Jewish-- against the Jewish laws and against the Jewish rituals or whatever.
And I remember the trouble start already brewing.
And-- and this was now after, we understand this was imported from Germany.
Sarny was 20,000 population.
Jews was 6,000.
About 6,000, to be-- I think this is the correct.
Some-- somewhat, you know, really with our neighbors some, you know, bad and good.
Some-- some weren't as-- you know, they hated.
They were a hating people.
So we had, you know, ups and downs with them.
But we didn't feel so bad.
The [INAUDIBLE], they were, you know, the students, the-- the-- the Jewish who went to our Polish school, they had-- they had a-- a worse feeling than I.
Because I went to a-- to a Hebrew, where mostly predominantly Jewish kids went on there.
To the end, yes.
To the end.
During the Soviets we had a radio.
And-- and then, of course, in 1939, war started with us.
War started with us in 1939.
And then, of course, Poland was split in two.
I remember the pact between the Germans and the--
There was a nonaggression pact.
The nonaggression pact--
--was signed between the Germans and the-- and the Russians.
And by this doing they split Poland.
And I fell into the jurisdiction of the Soviets.
I went to school.
I proceeded still as normal.
Then we start hearing right away some bad-- bad news.
But it did not affected us.
A lot of people started running from the other side, of the German side, wind up-- [NON-ENGLISH] we called them.
This is refugees, you know, to be-- to [? translated. ?] And they start telling us horrifying story.
But it did not affect us yet.
Felt safe, I went to school.
In-- and-- and we felt-- we felt all right.
My father, of course, could not, that changed completely different situation was already.
He-- he-- but he was working still in his same field.
He was a-- a director they made.
They gave him a title in there, with a lot of people under his supervision, whatever.
And-- and then, of course--
Oh yeah, I remember distinctly and very well.
It was the day-- it was a warm day, a hot day, I remember.
And we did not till the-- till the afternoon that the war started.
Actually, they attacked the Soviets with no warning whatsoever.
They attacked.
And within a week, we were overrun by the Germans.
And I remember we were very fearful.
Because some penetrated to us news that-- and I begged, I remember, my parents.
Let's run together with the Soviets.
Let's retreat with them.
And I remember my father said, whatever will be with [NON-ENGLISH] will be with [NON-ENGLISH].
It means whatever will be with the-- with the-- with the-- with the older Jews will be with me.
And he-- he was a very-- he was a educated man.
He spoke-- you know, he spoke German fluently.
He was an interpreter during the First World War, as I understand.
It was the Russian, or I don't know.
In the Russian, I would say, Polish and Russian, I don't know.
It was no time for it.
It was so-- such a fast-- doing it was so fast done, it was you had to decide [BACKGROUND NOISE] within a day or two.
How do I remember?
We were in the village.
And-- and some of the persons gave us hopes, don't worry.
You know, stay, we will protect you.
But this was just words.
And then did you-- did you ask me?
To Sarny, never heard it.
Because we were already still in-- in Rudnia, in-- in the village.
But we knew.
They didn't come exactly to Rudnia.
They didn't.
It was not a way for them to go through.
Because it was away in the-- in the woods, and away-- it's a village.
But we heard.
And we heard some bad news.
Some of them, they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, some of the girls, the first line of the soldiers, came in and they-- they attacked some Jewish girls.
And they-- what do they call?
They raped them.
They raped them?
Yes.
And-- and that's-- and with no time, a decree came out.
They let us stay for a little while.
That all the Jews must congregate, must come into Sarny, from the villages, from all the small towns, from the villages in particular, and my grandparents.
Through the Jewish Kehilla, through the Jewish council, through the Jewish council.
Yeah.
Somebody came.
And they were in communication with us.
--when the war started, in 1941.
What happened?
We moved in, in the ghetto, with-- we moved in, in the ghetto with some of our distant family, also Borko was their name.
And we lived in three small rooms with about 14 people living down there, with a little kitchen that we used-- used to share.
Was--?
Not-- we were-- not in our case.
It was fenced in by a fence, a-- a wooden fence.
It was not barbed wire.
But it was a wooden fence.
But nobody was allowed, of course, to go across the fence except through the gate.
If somebody would jump through a gate, they would be killed.
Because there was stationed some policemen, Ukrainians, Ukrainians and some Germans, but mostly Ukrainians, the [NON-ENGLISH], I call them.
Yes.
Every family had to come to the-- to the municipality, to the self-govern, to the Kehilla, they called it, you know, to the council.
They had to come, they had to produce every family has to produce two-- two or one person, almost twice a week or three times a week.
And they used to send them to a manual-- and stupid work, whatever had to be done.
The ghetto lasted from 1941 to 1942.
[SIGHS] Our ghetto was at one point surrounded.
We heard some news penetrated in little-- little cut-outs from the newspapers, what they-- they-- for the [? populace, ?] not for the ghetto people, for the population outside.
They are transferring Jews from this and this town.
And then we find out that there is-- was already transferring Jews.
It's a bad news.
It's-- they are killing them.
They are doing something with them, and we could not figure out.
But we knew that something horrible things are happening.
Next, our ghetto was surrounded one night.
And what-- right away a decree, right in the [? thing, ?] a big placard, a placard came.
It was written right in the-- on the front of the municipality that the-- all the [? populace ?] of the ghetto must come to the gate, to the square, where the gate was the next day.
Our ghetto was surrounded with-- with Ukrainians.
The-- not the-- the Ukrainians who were helping the Germans.
They were Nazis just as much, and Germans.
And on that day, people start-- we saw it's a panic, a panic right away.
Women with children start running from street to street.
And they used to-- just spiteful, they used to throw in-- used to with-- with real live ammunition, they used to throw 'em into the crowd.
And some of them were killed right there and then.
And my mother, right away we were-- we were a little bit away from the-- from the main crowd.
My mother insisted I should go into hiding.
I didn't want to.
But she absolutely insisted.
So I decided to go into hiding.
She said, maybe-- maybe some of-- maybe one of us will survive or something, whatever.
Go into hiding.
And the hiding place was at my uncle's house.
It was a barn for cows.
It was a double wall erected.
And there a little opening, you open up.
You went in down there.
And you close.
You masqueraded.
And then if a soldier or somebody would walk in, he wouldn't see nothing.
Because we were-- on the-- the double wall was-- in between it in the double wall.
Then I walked in there, but eight or nine people were sitting down there.
It was inside the ghetto, yeah.
But belonged to my uncle's mother-in-law or whatever.
Yeah, they were.
We were in fear every day.
And one day, we heard that they took some people from our ghetto to build some kind of camp, a camp in the outskirts of our town.
It was like in a-- in a little valley.
It was surrounded by little hills.
We were-- didn't have mountains, hills.
And it was in a little valley.
And they built down there a camp.
Right away, we-- we were suspicious.
The people who were building, asking the-- the Germans who were supervising them, what is it for.
They said we are-- have to bring prisoners of war from the front in there.
And that's where we are going to keep them.
And we-- we didn't have no choice to believe them.
And they deceived us.
This was camp was erected for the people of our town, plus the people from all the small towns.
And I'm speaking 6,000 Jewish inhabitants of ours, plus between 16 about 17,000 people, I can be shy a few hundred.
They brought them in in the cattle trains, whatever we saw on television, at night from the small towns.
It was close to [INAUDIBLE], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE].
From all the small town, they brought them in-- into Sarny.
And graves were already prepared.
They took them in groups, each town.
They took each town separately across the-- the hill, and Germans.
When we walked in that day, Germans were sitting right on the hill.
That's the camp.
If-- this is the answer to the camp that the people were building.
That was in 1942, after the ghetto was, of course-- after the ghetto was surrounded, and-- yes.
After the ghetto was surrounded, the next day.
The next day, you know, we send out a man, one of our people.
We were about-- about-- what did I say?
About how many people that were there in this-- in this hiding?
So we sent out a man to find out what's going on.
When he came back, a Mr.
Loria ?], a very respected, nice gentleman.
He was a-- he was a businessman before the war, and quite a well-to-do man.
And he came running back.
And he said they are [? appealing-- ?] they-- we must come-- come to the-- to the-- to the-- to the square.
If we are not, anybody who they'll find hiding, they will kill him on the spot.
We-- we didn't, at that time, we didn't know that the verdict was the same for this one.
I decided to-- to-- to-- to meet up with my family, with my-- with my family.
And I left them, and I went to-- with my family.
That was tough.
We were, like I described you, in three-- there were three little rooms, 14 people, and the fear, [? everything ?] was horrible.
At night, I remember we were not far from the gate of the ghetto.
And at night the-- the people, the Christian people of not Jewish persuasion, had homes in the ghetto.
So they got-- they were-- they left it-- they left, to leave down there.
They didn't have to move out.
And so at night, the people who used-- they did-- we had a curfew, 8 o'clock.
They didn't have no curfew.
So at night, we used to hear some trucks would come in, into the ghetto.
So we didn't know if this is a truck that was coming in to-- to stop at the house to drag out people to be killed or whatever it is.
But all they were-- some-- the truck belonged to a Christian family who was coming at night around 10:00, 11 o'clock from his work and going back home.
And in this fear, that's the way we lived.
In-- what do you mean, to the camp-- the camp?
Was-- was-- we were taken out from the ghetto.
You know, we had to come to the square of the ghetto each, the whole-- all the people from Sarny ghetto had to come.
And then there was a guy, a German, and I will never forget his-- his face.
He was standing with a little table in front of him.
And he was alphabetically calling out names, putting them into-- into groups of 30-40, putting them behind the gate, and right away be surrounded by Germans and Ukrainians.
And they take them away right away someplace.
I, my family name was Szyr.
So I was almost at the end to leave the ghetto.
That's exactly.
They took us down there to be executed.
OK.
